findings.title: JWT signed with a weak secret
findings.description: The HMAC secret was recovered ('secret') from a small wordlist, so tokens can be forged.
findings.exploit: Sign a token with elevated claims (e.g. role=admin) using the cracked secret.
findings.fix: Rotate to a long, random secret (>=256 bits) stored securely.

findings.title: SQL injection (error-based)
findings.description: Injecting into 'name' produced a database error, proving unsanitised input reaches the SQL engine.
findings.evidence: param 'name' payload "'" -> DB error: near "'": syntax error
findings.exploit: An attacker can extract or modify data via name, e.g. UNION-based extraction or boolean/time blind exfiltration.
findings.fix: Use parameterised queries / prepared statements for this parameter.

poc.curl: curl -i -X GET 'http://127.0.0.1:8723/users?name=1%27' -H 'host: 127.0.0.1:8723' -H 'accept: */*' -H 'accept-encoding: gzip, deflate' -H 'connection: keep-alive' -H 'user-agent: Argus/0.1 (+https://github.com/Sarthak-47/ARGUS)' -H 'cookie: ***REDACTED***'
poc.request:
GET http://127.0.0.1:8723/users?name=1%27
host: 127.0.0.1:8723
accept: */*
accept-encoding: gzip, deflate
connection: keep-alive
user-agent: Argus/0.1 (+https://github.com/Sarthak-47/ARGUS)
cookie: ***REDACTED***
poc.response:
HTTP 500
SQL syntax error: near "'": syntax error in SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '1''

findings.title: CORS misconfiguration (arbitrary origin)
findings.description: The server reflects an attacker-controlled Origin with credentials enabled, allowing malicious sites to read authenticated responses cross-origin.
findings.evidence: Origin: https://evil.argus-test.example -> Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.argus-test.example; Allow-Credentials: true
findings.exploit: Host a page on a hostile origin that fetches the API with credentials and exfiltrates the response.
findings.fix: Reflect Origin only from a strict allow-list; never combine '*' or reflected Origin with Allow-Credentials: true.

findings.title: CORS misconfiguration (null origin)
findings.description: The server reflects an attacker-controlled Origin with credentials enabled, allowing malicious sites to read authenticated responses cross-origin.
findings.evidence: Origin: null -> Access-Control-Allow-Origin: null; Allow-Credentials: true
findings.exploit: Host a page on a hostile origin that fetches the API with credentials and exfiltrates the response.
findings.fix: Reflect Origin only from a strict allow-list; never combine '*' or reflected Origin with Allow-Credentials: true.

findings.title: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
findings.description: Object identifiers (path segment) can be enumerated to read other objects with no ownership/authorization check.
findings.evidence: enumerating the path segment returned distinct objects without authentication
findings.exploit: Increment/replace the identifier to access other users' records.
findings.fix: Enforce per-object ownership/authorization on every access; use unguessable IDs.

findings.title: Path traversal (arbitrary file read)
findings.description: The 'file' parameter is used to build a file path without normalisation, allowing reads outside the intended directory.
findings.exploit: Read /etc/passwd, source code, secrets or config files.
findings.fix: Normalise the resolved path and verify it stays within an allowed base dir; use an allow-list of filenames.

findings.title: Reflected XSS
findings.description: Input to 'q' is reflected into the HTML response without encoding, so injected markup executes in the victim's browser.
findings.evidence: param 'q' reflected unencoded: <argusb11070> appears verbatim in HTML
findings.exploit: Deliver a crafted link; the script runs in the victim's session (cookie theft, session hijack, actions on their behalf).
findings.fix: Context-encode output (HTML-escape), and apply a strict Content-Security-Policy.

poc.curl: curl -i -X GET 'http://127.0.0.1:8723/search?q=%3Cargusb11070%3E%22%27' -H 'host: 127.0.0.1:8723' -H 'accept: */*' -H 'accept-encoding: gzip, deflate' -H 'connection: keep-alive' -H 'user-agent: Argus/0.1 (+https://github.com/Sarthak-47/ARGUS)' -H 'cookie: ***REDACTED***'
poc.request:
GET http://127.0.0.1:8723/search?q=%3Cargusb11070%3E%22%27
host: 127.0.0.1:8723
accept: */*
accept-encoding: gzip, deflate
connection: keep-alive
user-agent: Argus/0.1 (+https://github.com/Sarthak-47/ARGUS)
cookie: ***REDACTED***

findings.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (blind)
findings.description: The server fetched an attacker-supplied URL via 'url', confirmed by an out-of-band callback.
findings.evidence: param 'url' caused the server to request our callback host
findings.exploit: Reach internal services or cloud metadata (169.254.169.254) to steal credentials and pivot.
findings.fix: Allow-list outbound hosts; block link-local and private ranges; disable redirects.

findings.title: Clickjacking — no framing protection
findings.description: The page can be embedded in a hostile iframe, enabling clickjacking (UI redress) attacks against authenticated users.
findings.evidence: no X-Frame-Options and no CSP frame-ancestors directive
findings.exploit: Frame the page on an attacker site and trick users into clicking hidden controls.
findings.fix: Send X-Frame-Options: DENY (or SAMEORIGIN) and/or CSP frame-ancestors 'none'.

findings.title: Form without CSRF token
findings.description: A state-changing form carries no anti-CSRF token, so a forged cross-site request can perform the action on a victim's behalf.
findings.evidence: POST form, hidden fields: ['amount'] — no CSRF token
findings.exploit: Auto-submit the form from an attacker page while the victim is logged in.
findings.fix: Include and validate a per-session anti-CSRF token; use SameSite cookies.

findings.title: GraphQL introspection enabled
findings.description: Schema introspection is enabled, disclosing every type, query and mutation — a roadmap of the entire API for an attacker.
findings.exploit: Enumerate the full schema, then target sensitive mutations/queries.
findings.fix: Disable introspection in production; restrict it to trusted environments.

findings.title: Improper input handling / error disclosure
findings.description: A malformed value (oversized) in 'n' caused an unhandled error exposing internal details.
findings.evidence: param 'n' + oversized payload -> HTTP 500 with stack trace/exception
findings.exploit: Malformed input can crash handlers or leak stack traces aiding further attacks.
findings.fix: Validate and constrain input types/length; return generic errors; never leak stack traces.

findings.title: JWT has no expiry (exp) claim
findings.description: The JWT lacks an 'exp' claim, so a leaked token is valid forever.
findings.exploit: A stolen token can be replayed indefinitely.
findings.fix: Add a short 'exp' and validate it server-side.

findings.title: Session cookie missing security flags
findings.description: The session cookie 'session' is missing HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite, exposing it to theft or CSRF.
findings.exploit: Cookie theft via XSS (no HttpOnly) or interception (no Secure).
findings.fix: Set HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite=Lax/Strict on session cookies.

findings.title: Missing security headers
findings.description: The application does not set content-security-policy, x-frame-options, strict-transport-security, x-content-type-options, weakening defence-in-depth against XSS/clickjacking/MITM.
findings.fix: Set the missing headers (CSP, X-Frame-Options, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options).
